title: Immigration and migration in the Gilded Age
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on immigration and migration in the Gilded Age.
topic_path: Immigration and migration in the Gilded Age | 6.8
Immigration and migration in the Gilded Age

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

Year | Number of gainful workers | Percentage of paid workers who are foreign-born | Percentage of paid workers who are women
- | - | - | - 
1870 | 12,506,000 | 21.6% | 14.8%
1880 | 17,392,000 | 20.1% | 15.2%
1890 | 23,318,000 | 22.0% | 17.0%
1900 | 29,073,000 | 19.7% | 18.1%

**The main trend shown in the table was most directly associated with which of the following processes occurring in the United States at the time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in industrialization and increase in factory work
increased migration to government-provided land in the Midwest
replacement of factory workers with labor-saving machines
regulations on businesses to meet labor demands

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

Year | Number of gainful workers | Percentage of paid workers who are foreign-born | Percentage of paid workers who are women
- | - | - | - 
1870 | 12,506,000 | 21.6% | 14.8%
1880 | 17,392,000 | 20.1% | 15.2%
1890 | 23,318,000 | 22.0% | 17.0%
1900 | 29,073,000 | 19.7% | 18.1%

**Which of the following best describes the overall demographic trend shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the expansion of the workforce because of massive industrialization
the rise in immigration following a lack of job opportunities in Europe
the ratification of an equal rights amendment to support women in the workforce
the solidification of gender roles as immigrant men replaced women in factories

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

Year | Number of gainful workers | Percentage of paid workers who are foreign-born | Percentage of paid workers who are women
- | - | - | - 
1870 | 12,506,000 | 21.6% | 14.8%
1880 | 17,392,000 | 20.1% | 15.2%
1890 | 23,318,000 | 22.0% | 17.0%
1900 | 29,073,000 | 19.7% | 18.1%

**The graph most strongly supports which of the following arguments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As job opportunities increased, the workforce expanded beyond native-born men.
As factories industrialized, businesses deployed new machines that replaced workers.
As more factories closed down, families migrated to rural areas to farm and earn some money.
As machines replaced workers, factory owners lowered the average wages for workers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Foreign-born population of American cities, 1870-1900** ||
City | 1870 population | Percent foreign-born | 1900 population | Percent foreign-born
- | - | - | - | - 
New York City | 1,478,103 | 28% | 3,437,202 | 35%
Philadelphia | 1,293,687 | 14% | 1,350,000 | 22%
Chicago | 298,977 | 48% | 1,698,575 | 35%
Boston | 250,525 | 35% | 560,892 | 35%
Los Angeles | 5,728 | 35% | 102,479 | 19%


**Which of the following push factors was a significant cause of the trend from 1870 to 1900 shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: poverty and limited economic opportunities
racial and ethnic discrimination
civil wars against corrupt governments
environmental disasters that led to famine

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Foreign-born population of American cities, 1870-1900** ||
City | 1870 population | Percent foreign-born | 1900 population | Percent foreign-born
- | - | - | - | - 
New York City | 1,478,103 | 28% | 3,437,202 | 35%
Philadelphia | 1,293,687 | 14% | 1,350,000 | 22%
Chicago | 298,977 | 48% | 1,698,575 | 35%
Boston | 250,525 | 35% | 560,892 | 35%
Los Angeles | 5,728 | 35% | 102,479 | 19%

**Which of the following best explains the changes in the foreign-born population from 1870 to 1900?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Immigration to major cities increased along with mass internal migration.
Immigration increased to cities in the Midwest, not the Northeast.
Immigrants mostly relocated to the western half of the United States .
Immigrants sought to work on farms, not work in factories.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Population change in cities, 1870-1900** ||
City | 1870 population | 1900 population | Percent change
- | - | - | -
New York City | 1,478,103 | 3,437,202 | 265%
Philadelphia | 1,293,687 | 1,350,000 | 4%
Chicago | 298,977 | 1,698,575 | 468%
Boston | 250,525 | 560,892 | 124%
Los Angeles | 5,728 | 102,479 | 1689%

**Which of the following was a significant cause of the trend in migration from 1870 to 1900 shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: job opportunities in urban areas
tenement housing in cities
religious persecution
sharecropping in the South

Examine the table and answer the question below.

**The major trend depicted in the table most directly reflects which of the following developments in the United States in the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in urbanization
the growth of industrialization
the restrictions on immigration
the effects of westward migration

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“Although the effects of immigrants on the composition of the U.S. population were apparent by the census of 1870, it was the dramatic shift, between the 1880s and 1900s, in the sources of European immigration to the United States from northwestern countries to southeastern ones that excited and sustained public debate and comment. In 1882 . . . 13.1 percent came from southern and eastern European countries. . . . In 1907, these countries supplied 81 percent of a total of 1,207,619 European immigrants.” 

>-Source: Desmond King, *Making Americans: Immigration, Race, and the Origins of the Diverse Democracy,* 2009

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: economic opportunities in major US cities
offers of government loans for people willing to relocate
start of the first major world war in Italy and Russia
relaxation of immigration laws in the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“Although the effects of immigrants on the composition of the U.S. population were apparent by the census of 1870, it was the dramatic shift, between the 1880s and 1900s, in the sources of European immigration to the United States from northwestern countries to southeastern ones that excited and sustained public debate and comment. In 1882 . . . 13.1 percent came from southern and eastern European countries. . . . In 1907, these countries supplied 81 percent of a total of 1,207,619 European immigrants.” 

>-Source: Desmond King, *Making Americans: Immigration, Race, and the Origins of the Diverse Democracy,* 2009

**Which of the following most likely contributed to the increase in immigration from southern and eastern Europe referred to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: religious persecution and widespread poverty in European states
passage of an immigration quota act that favored European immigrants
declaration of the Monroe Doctrine, which eased immigration restrictions
resurgence of American agriculture as other countries industrialized

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The foreign-born residents of Chicago and of other large cities of the country tend to segregate themselves in separate national groups where, in churches and schools, and in social, fraternal, and national organizations, the speech, the ideals, and to some extent the manner of life of the mother country are zealously preserved and guarded. . . . These children are held in a sense to a double standard; they are inevitably drawn to the American manners and customs which they meet in the school, on the street, and in the factory, while in their own homes the old European standards of life are maintained.”

>-Source: Edith Abbott and Sophonisba Preston Breckinridge, “Chapter 3: The Child of the Immigrant,” *The Delinquent Child and the Home,* 1917

**The excerpt provided is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in immigration from southern and eastern Europe
the development of settlement houses
the emergence of a public school movement
the creation of a social security program

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The foreign-born residents of Chicago and of other large cities of the country tend to segregate themselves in separate national groups where, in churches and schools, and in social, fraternal, and national organizations, the speech, the ideals, and to some extent the manner of life of the mother country are zealously preserved and guarded. . . . These children are held in a sense to a double standard; they are inevitably drawn to the American manners and customs which they meet in the school, on the street, and in the factory, while in their own homes the old European standards of life are maintained.”

>-Source: Edith Abbott and Sophonisba Preston Breckinridge, “Chapter 3: The Child of the Immigrant,” *The Delinquent Child and the Home,* 1917

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the developments described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: expansion of economic opportunities in factories
reduction in federal citizenship requirements
creation of tenement housing in major cities
rise of political machines to influence local politics

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“The foreign-born residents of Chicago and of other large cities of the country tend to segregate themselves in separate national groups where, in churches and schools, and in social, fraternal, and national organizations, the speech, the ideals, and to some extent the manner of life of the mother country are zealously preserved and guarded. . . . These children are held in a sense to a double standard; they are inevitably drawn to the American manners and customs which they meet in the school, on the street, and in the factory, while in their own homes the old European standards of life are maintained.”

>-Source: Edith Abbott and Sophonisba Preston Breckinridge, “Chapter 3: The Child of the Immigrant,” *The Delinquent Child and the Home,* 1917

**The immigrant neighborhoods described in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following characteristics of American society during the period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a rise in cultural pluralism in major cities
an increase in the size of the middle class
a surge in conflicts over imperialism
a decrease in urban political machines
